q: Blocks of masses m, 2m, 4m and 8m are arranged in a line of a frictionless floor. Another block of mass m , moving with speed υ along the same line (see figure) collides with mass m in perfectly inelastic manner. All the subsequent collisions are also perfectly inelastic. By the time the last block of mass 8m starts moving the total energy loss is p% of the original energy. Value of ‘p ’ is close to:
question: Blocks of masses m, 2m, 4m and 8m are arranged in a line of a frictionless floor. Another block of mass m , moving with speed υ along the same line (see figure) collides with mass m in perfectly inelastic manner. All the subsequent collisions are also perfectly inelastic. By the time the last block of mass 8m starts moving the total energy loss is p% of the original energy. Value of ‘p ’ is close to:
solution:
Inelastic collision

mv=16 mv1

ΔK loss=12mv2−1216Mv162

=12mv2−12Mv216

=12mv21516

% ΔK loss=12mv2151612MV2×100=1516×100=93.75%

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A ball is dropped from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the coefficient of restitution for the collision between ball and floor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, after hitting the floor, the ball rebounds to a height of ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: A ball is dropped from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the coefficient of restitution for the collision between ball and floor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, after hitting the floor, the ball rebounds to a height of ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
As we know h'=e2h, where h'=height after the collision, h=height before the collision and e=coefficient of restitution.

Therefore,

h'=0.52×20 m=5 m

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> at rest explodes into three pieces, in the ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Two smaller pieces fly off perpendicular to each other with velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The velocity of the third piece will be
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> at rest explodes into three pieces, in the ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Two smaller pieces fly off perpendicular to each other with velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The velocity of the third piece will be
solution:
Let the masses of the three parts be m', m' and 2m'.

Momentum of first part p1=m'×30=30m' and momentum of second part p2=m'×40=40m'.

Third part will fly in a direction opposite to the direction of resultant of p1 and p2, so that total momentum of the system after explosion remains zero.

Momentum of the third part is p3=p12+p22=30m'2+40m'2=50m'

Velocity of third part, v=50m'2m'=25 m s-1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Two bodies are having kinetic energies in the ratio 16:9. If they have same linear momentum, the ratio of their masses respectively is:
question: Two bodies are having kinetic energies in the ratio 16:9. If they have same linear momentum, the ratio of their masses respectively is:
solution:
The kinetic energy and the momentum of a moving object are related by

K=p22m   ...1

Hence, the ratio of the kinetic energies of two objects having masses m and m' can be calculated as follows:

KK'=p22mp22m'=m'm   ...2

Substitute the ratio of the kinetic energies into equation (2) and simplify to obtain the ratio of their masses.

⇒169=m'm⇒m:m'=9:16

q: Two particles of equal mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> have respective initial velocities <math><mi>u</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math><mi>u</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> . They collide completely inelastically. The energy lost in the process is:
question: Two particles of equal mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> have respective initial velocities <math><mi>u</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math><mi>u</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> . They collide completely inelastically. The energy lost in the process is:
solution:
Conserving momentum
mui^+mu2i^+u2j^=2mu1i^+u2j^
On solving
u1=3u4 and u2=u4
Change in K.E.
12mu2+12mu222-122m9u216+u216
=3mu24-5mu28=mu28

q: A machine gun of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> fires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> bullets at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn></math> bullets per minute with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> each. The recoil velocity of the gun is :
question: A machine gun of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> fires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> bullets at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn></math> bullets per minute with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> each. The recoil velocity of the gun is :
solution:
Number of bullets fired per second 18060=3.

Applying momentum conservation, we get

320×10-3×100=10v

⇒v=0.6 m s-1

q: Particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with another particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> which is at rest initially. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the velocities of particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> after collision respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and after collision <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>−</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
question: Particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with another particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> which is at rest initially. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the velocities of particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> after collision respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and after collision <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>−</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
solution:
m1u→1+m2u→2=m1v→1+m2v→2

2m2(3i^+j^)+m2×0=2m2(i^+3j^)+m2×v→2

23i^+2j^=2i^+23j^+v→2

v→2=(3-1)i^-(3-1)j^

v→1=i^+3j^

Angle between v→1 and v→2 is 105°

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
The position of an object having mass $0.1$ kg as a function of time $t$ is given as $\vec{r} = \left(10t^2\hat{i} + 5t^3\hat{j}\right)$ m. At $t = 1$ s, which of the following statements are correct?
A. The linear momentum $\vec{p} = \left(2\hat{i} + 1.5\hat{j}\right)$ kg·m/s.
B. The force acting on the object $\vec{F} = \left(2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}\right)$ N.
C. The angular momentum of the object about its origin $\vec{L} = 15\hat{k}$ J·s.
D. The torque acting on the object about its origin $\vec{\tau} = 20\hat{k}$ N·m.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
The position of an object having mass $0.1$ kg as a function of time $t$ is given as $\vec{r} = \left(10t^2\hat{i} + 5t^3\hat{j}\right)$ m. At $t = 1$ s, which of the following statements are correct?
A. The linear momentum $\vec{p} = \left(2\hat{i} + 1.5\hat{j}\right)$ kg·m/s.
B. The force acting on the object $\vec{F} = \left(2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}\right)$ N.
C. The angular momentum of the object about its origin $\vec{L} = 15\hat{k}$ J·s.
D. The torque acting on the object about its origin $\vec{\tau} = 20\hat{k}$ N·m.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Given the position vector $\vec{r} = 10t^2\hat{i} + 5t^3\hat{j}$

Velocity $\vec{v} = \dfrac{d\vec{r}}{dt} = 20t\hat{i} + 15t^2\hat{j}$

Acceleration $\vec{a} = \dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = 20\hat{i} + 30t\hat{j}$

At $t = 1$ s:
$\vec{r} = 10\hat{i} + 5\hat{j}$ m
$\vec{v} = 20\hat{i} + 15\hat{j}$ m/s
$\vec{a} = 20\hat{i} + 30\hat{j}$ m/s$^2$

Linear momentum $\vec{p} = m\vec{v} = 0.1(20\hat{i} + 15\hat{j}) = 2\hat{i} + 1.5\hat{j}$ kg$\cdot$m/s (Statement A is correct)

Force $\vec{F} = m\vec{a} = 0.1(20\hat{i} + 30\hat{j}) = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}$ N (Statement B is correct)

Angular momentum $\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p} = (10\hat{i} + 5\hat{j}) \times (2\hat{i} + 1.5\hat{j}) = (10 \times 1.5 - 5 \times 2)\hat{k} = 5\hat{k}$ J$\cdot$s (Statement C is incorrect)

Torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} = (10\hat{i} + 5\hat{j}) \times (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}) = (10 \times 3 - 5 \times 2)\hat{k} = 20\hat{k}$ N$\cdot$m (Statement D is correct)

Therefore, statements A, B, and D are correct.

Answer: A, B and D only

q: The block of mass $M$ moving on the frictionless horizontal surface collides with a spring of spring constant $\mathrm{K}$ and compresses it by length $\mathrm{L}$. The maximum momentum of the block after collision is
question: The block of mass $M$ moving on the frictionless horizontal surface collides with a spring of spring constant $\mathrm{K}$ and compresses it by length $\mathrm{L}$. The maximum momentum of the block after collision is
solution: $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{KL}^2=\frac{\mathrm{P}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \quad \therefore \mathrm{P}=\sqrt{\mathrm{MK}} \mathrm{L}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A particle of mass m is projected with a speed u from the ground at an angle <math><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> w.r.t. horizontal (x-axis). When it has reached its maximum height, it collides completely inelastically with another particle of the same mass and velocity <math><mi>u</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> The horizontal distance covered by the combined mass before reaching the ground is:
question: A particle of mass m is projected with a speed u from the ground at an angle <math><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> w.r.t. horizontal (x-axis). When it has reached its maximum height, it collides completely inelastically with another particle of the same mass and velocity <math><mi>u</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> The horizontal distance covered by the combined mass before reaching the ground is:
solution:
pi=pf
mu+mucosθ=2mv
⇒v=u1+cos⁡6002=34u
So horizontal range after collision =vt
=v2Hmaxg
=34u2u2sin2⁡6002g2
=34u234g=33u28g

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving at speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> collides elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> at rest. After the collision, the two masses move at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with respect to the initial direction of motion of the body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math>. The largest possible value of the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>M</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> for which the angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be equal, is :
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving at speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> collides elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> at rest. After the collision, the two masses move at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with respect to the initial direction of motion of the body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math>. The largest possible value of the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>M</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> for which the angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be equal, is :
solution:
Linear momentum conservation
(i) y-direction
mv2sinθ=mv1sinθ
mv2=mv1           ...(1)
(ii) x-direction
mv0=mv2cosθ+mv1cosθ            ...(2)
from (1) and (2)
mv0=mv2cosθ+mv2cosθ
mv0=2mv2cosθ       ...(3)
(iii) e=1given
v2-v1cos2θv0cosθ=1
v2-v1cos2θ=v0cosθ       ...(4)
v2-m22Mcos2θ=v0cosθ
v21-mMcos2θ=v0cosθ
mv02mcosθ1-mMcos2θ=v0cosθ
M-mcos2θ=2mcos2θ
M=2mcos2θ+m2cos2θ-1
Mm=4cos2θ-1
Mmmax=4-1=3

question: A rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> has non-uniform linear mass density given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>l</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math><mi>a</mi></math> and <math><mi>b</mi></math> are constants and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>l</mi></math> The value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> for the centre of mass of the rod is at:

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The center of mass of a thin rectangular plate (fig - x ) with sides of length $a$ and $b$, whose mass per unit area $(\sigma)$ varies as $\sigma=\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}$ (where $\sigma_0$ is a constant), would be $\qquad$
question: The center of mass of a thin rectangular plate (fig - x ) with sides of length $a$ and $b$, whose mass per unit area $(\sigma)$ varies as $\sigma=\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}$ (where $\sigma_0$ is a constant), would be $\qquad$
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & d m=\sigma d A \\ & \begin{aligned} d & =\sigma(d x)(d y)=\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}(d x)(d y) \\ x_{c o m} & =\frac{\int x d m}{\int d m}=\frac{\int x \frac{\left(\sigma_0 x\right)}{a b}(d x)(d y)}{\int_0 \frac{\sigma_0}{a b}(d x)(d y)} \\ & =\frac{\int_0^a x^2 d x \int_0^b d y}{\int_0^b x d x \int_0^b d y}=\frac{2 a}{3} \\ y_{c o m} & =\frac{\int y d m}{\int d m}=\frac{\int y\left(\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}\right)(d x)(d y)}{\int \frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}(d x)(d y)}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{\int_0^a x d x \int_0^b y d y}{\int_0^a x d x \int_0^b d y}=\frac{b}{2}$
i.e., $\vec{r}_{\mathrm{com}} \equiv\left(\frac{2 a}{3}, \frac{b}{2}\right)$

question: Consider a two particle system with particles having masses $m_1$ and $m_2$. If the first particle is pushed towards the centre of mass through a distance $d$, by what distance should the second particle be moved, so as to keep the centre of mass at the same position?

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The position of center of mass of three masses $2$ kg, $3$ kg and $15$ kg placed with respect to mid point ($p$) of normal bisector, as shown in the figure is _______.
question: The position of center of mass of three masses $2$ kg, $3$ kg and $15$ kg placed with respect to mid point ($p$) of normal bisector, as shown in the figure is _______.
solution:
Let the midpoint $p$ of the normal bisector be the origin $(0,0)$.

The triangle is isosceles with two sides of $10\text{ m}$ and an included angle of $120^\circ$. The altitude from the $15\text{ kg}$ mass bisects the angle into two $60^\circ$ angles.

The length of the altitude is $10 \cos(60^\circ) = 5\text{ m}$.

Since $p$ is the midpoint of this altitude, the $15\text{ kg}$ mass is at a distance of $2.5\text{ m}$ above $p$, and the base is $2.5\text{ m}$ below $p$.

The distance of the $2\text{ kg}$ and $3\text{ kg}$ masses from the altitude is $10 \sin(60^\circ) = 5\sqrt{3}\text{ m}$.

The coordinates of the masses with respect to $p$ are:
$m_1 = 15\text{ kg}$ at $(0, 2.5)$
$m_2 = 2\text{ kg}$ at $(-5\sqrt{3}, -2.5)$
$m_3 = 3\text{ kg}$ at $(5\sqrt{3}, -2.5)$

The x-coordinate of the center of mass is:
$x_{cm} = \dfrac{m_1 x_1 + m_2 x_2 + m_3 x_3}{m_1 + m_2 + m_3} = \dfrac{15(0) + 2(-5\sqrt{3}) + 3(5\sqrt{3})}{15 + 2 + 3} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{3}}{20} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}$

The y-coordinate of the center of mass is:
$y_{cm} = \dfrac{m_1 y_1 + m_2 y_2 + m_3 y_3}{m_1 + m_2 + m_3} = \dfrac{15(2.5) + 2(-2.5) + 3(-2.5)}{20} = \dfrac{37.5 - 12.5}{20} = \dfrac{25}{20} = 1.25$

The position of the center of mass is $\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}, 1.25\right)$.

Answer: $\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}, 1.25\right)$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
A pendulum of length 2 m consists of a wooden bob of mass 50 g. A bullet of mass 75 g is fired towards the stationary bob with a speed v. The bullet emerges out of the bob with a speed v3 and the bob just completes the vertical circle. The value of v is _____  m s-1

(if g=10 m s-2).
question:
A pendulum of length 2 m consists of a wooden bob of mass 50 g. A bullet of mass 75 g is fired towards the stationary bob with a speed v. The bullet emerges out of the bob with a speed v3 and the bob just completes the vertical circle. The value of v is _____  m s-1

(if g=10 m s-2).
solution:
For the bob to completer the vertical circle, vb=5gl.

From the conservation of momentum, 

m1v=m2vb+m1v3⇒75v=505gl+75v3

⇒752v3=505×10×2

⇒752v3=50×10

⇒v=500×3150=50050=10 m s-1

q: Two bodies of the same mass are moving with the same speed, but in different directions in a plane. They have a completely inelastic collision and move together thereafter with a final speed which is half of their initial velocities of the two bodies (in degree) is -
question: Two bodies of the same mass are moving with the same speed, but in different directions in a plane. They have a completely inelastic collision and move together thereafter with a final speed which is half of their initial velocities of the two bodies (in degree) is -
solution:
Momentum conservation along x direction

2mvcosθ=(2m)v2

cosθ=12

θ=60°

then angle between their initial velocities

= 120°

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A bullet of 10 g leaves the barrel of gun with a velocity of 600 m s-1. If the barrel of gun is 50 cm long and mass of gun is 3 kg, then value of impulse supplied to the gun will be:
question: A bullet of 10 g leaves the barrel of gun with a velocity of 600 m s-1. If the barrel of gun is 50 cm long and mass of gun is 3 kg, then value of impulse supplied to the gun will be:
solution:
The formula to calculate the impulse J on the gun is given by

J=mv-u   ...1

where, m is the mass, u is the initial velocity and v is the final velocity of the bullet.

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required impulse.

J=10 g×1 kg1000 g×600-0 m s-1= 6 N s

q: Two particles A and B of equal mass M are moving with the same speed v as shown in figure. They collide completely inelastic and move as a single particle C. The angle θ that the path of C makes with the X-axis is given by-
question: Two particles A and B of equal mass M are moving with the same speed v as shown in figure. They collide completely inelastic and move as a single particle C. The angle θ that the path of C makes with the X-axis is given by-
solution:
After inelastic collision mass of the combination is 2m

v' be the velocity of the combined mass

Using conservation of momentum along X and Y-axis

2mv′sin⁡θ=mv2+mv32

2mv′cos⁡θ=mv2-mv2

2sinθ2cosθ=12+3212-12=2+31-2   

tan θ=2+3 1- 2

The speed of each of the moving particle will be
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is moving with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mtext>v</mtext></math> and collides with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> moving with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>v</mtext></math> in the same direction. ⇒v1=v2=22v

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Three blocks A, B and C are lying on a smooth horizontal surface, as shown in the figure. A and B have equal masses, m while C has mass M. Block A is given an initial speed v towards B due to which it collides with B perfectly inelastically. The combined mass collides with C, also perfectly inelastically . 56th of the initial kinetic energy is lost in the whole process. What is the value of M/m?
question: Three blocks A, B and C are lying on a smooth horizontal surface, as shown in the figure. A and B have equal masses, m while C has mass M. Block A is given an initial speed v towards B due to which it collides with B perfectly inelastically. The combined mass collides with C, also perfectly inelastically . 56th of the initial kinetic energy is lost in the whole process. What is the value of M/m?
solution:
mv=m+mv′

m+mv′=m+m+Mvf

∴ final velocity vf=m2m+Mv

Initial energy =12mv2

Final energy  =122m+Mmv2m+M2

Given that 5612mv2=12mv2-122m+Mmv2m+M2

112mv2=12m2v22m+M

6m=2m+M

Mm=4

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A uniform thin metal plate of mass $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ with dimensions is shown. The ratio of $\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}$ coordinates of center of mass of plate in $\frac{n}{9}$. The value of $n$ is ________
question: A uniform thin metal plate of mass $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ with dimensions is shown. The ratio of $\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}$ coordinates of center of mass of plate in $\frac{n}{9}$. The value of $n$ is ________
solution:
$\mathrm{m}_1=\sigma \times 5=10 \mathrm{Kg}$

$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{x}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{x}_2=\mathrm{m}_3 \mathrm{x}_3 \\ & 10 \mathrm{x}_1+2(1.5)=12(1.5) \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_1=1.5 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{y}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{y}_2=\mathrm{m}_3 \mathrm{y}_3 \\ & 10 \mathrm{y}_1+2(1.5)=12 \times 1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}_1=0.9 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{x}_1}{\mathrm{y}_1}=\frac{1.5}{0.9}=\frac{15}{9} \\ & \mathrm{n}=15\end{aligned}$

q: A uniform rod of mass 250 g having length 100 cm is balanced on a sharp edge at 40 cm mark. A mass of 400 g is suspended at 10 cm mark. To maintain the balance of the rod, the mass to be suspended at 90 cm mark, is
question: A uniform rod of mass 250 g having length 100 cm is balanced on a sharp edge at 40 cm mark. A mass of 400 g is suspended at 10 cm mark. To maintain the balance of the rod, the mass to be suspended at 90 cm mark, is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \tau_{\text {Net }}=0 \Rightarrow(400 \mathrm{~g} \times 30)=(250 \mathrm{~g} \times 10)(\mathrm{mg} \times 50) \\ & \mathrm{m}=\frac{12000-2500}{50}=\frac{9500}{50} \\ & \mathrm{M}=190 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$

q: Two blocks of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are placed on the same straight line with coordinates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moved on the same line through a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> towards the other block. The distance through which the block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> must be moved to keep the position of centre of mass of the system unchanged is
question: Two blocks of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are placed on the same straight line with coordinates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moved on the same line through a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> towards the other block. The distance through which the block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> must be moved to keep the position of centre of mass of the system unchanged is
solution:
Initial position of centre of mass: xCM=100+30×x40=3x4

Final position of the centre of mass: x'CM=106+30×x-y40=6+3x-y4

As position of centre of mass remains same,

xCM=x'CM ⇒3x4=6+3x-y4⇒3x=6+3x-3y  ⇒y=2 cm

Alternate method, ∆xCOM=m1∆x1+m2∆x2m1+m2⇒0=10×6+30y⇒y=-2 cm

q: A square shaped hole of side l= a2 is carved out at a distance  d=a2 from the centre 'O' of a uniform circular disk of radius a. If the distance of the centre of mass of the remaining portion from O is -ax, value of X (to the nearest integer) is :
question: A square shaped hole of side l= a2 is carved out at a distance  d=a2 from the centre 'O' of a uniform circular disk of radius a. If the distance of the centre of mass of the remaining portion from O is -ax, value of X (to the nearest integer) is :
solution:
Xa=A·X-A·X1A-A1=πa2×0-a24×a2πa2-a24
=-a3/8π-14a2=-a2(4π-1)=-a8π-2=-a23
x=23

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> collides with a stationary particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math>. After collision, they stick together and continue to move together with velocity
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> collides with a stationary particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math>. After collision, they stick together and continue to move together with velocity
solution:
The law of conservation of momentum is given as

m1u+m2u'=m1+m2v'

The given data is 

u=vu'=0 m s-1m1=mm2=2m

Using the conservation of momentum equation,

mv+2m(0)=(m+2m)v'⇒v'=mv3m=v3

q:
A rod of length 5 L is bent right angle keeping one side length as 2 L.

The position of the centre of mass of the system: (Consider $\mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$)
question:
A rod of length 5 L is bent right angle keeping one side length as 2 L.

The position of the centre of mass of the system: (Consider $\mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$)
solution:
$\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{com}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~m}(10)+3 \mathrm{~m}(0)}{5 \mathrm{~m}}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{com}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~m}(0)+3 \mathrm{~m}(15)}{5 \mathrm{~m}}=9 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{com}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+9 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$

The speed of the two body centre of mass is $x/10$. Find the value of x.
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> makes an elastic collision with another body at rest and continues to move in the original direction but with one fourth of its initial speed. The speed of the two body centre of mass is $x/10$. Find the value of x.
solution:
pi=pf

2×4=2×1+m2×v2

m2v2=6 …(1)

By coefficient of restitution, 
1=v2-14⇒v2=5 m s-1

by (i)

m2×5=6

m2=1.2 kg

vcm=m1v1+m2v2 m1+m2

vcm=2×1+1.2×52+1.2=83.2=2510

x=25

q: Two particles of masses M and 2M are moving with speeds of 10 m s-1 and 5 m s-1, as shown in the figure. They collide at the origin and after that they move along the indicated directions with speeds v1  and  v2, respectively. The values of v1 and  v2 are, nearly
question: Two particles of masses M and 2M are moving with speeds of 10 m s-1 and 5 m s-1, as shown in the figure. They collide at the origin and after that they move along the indicated directions with speeds v1  and  v2, respectively. The values of v1 and  v2 are, nearly
solution:
From conservation of linear momentum,
Along x-axis:

10Mcos300+52Mcos450=Mv1cos300+2Mv2cos450
10 M 32+ 10M12=M32 v1+2Mv212
32 v1+2v2=5(3+2) … (1)
Along y-axis:
-10M12+10M12=Mv112-2Mv212
 v12-2v2=5(2-1) … (2)
From equations (1) and (2)
v132+12=53+2+5(2-1)
v1=13.02 m/s
v2=53+52-13.02×322
=19.72 m s-1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The figure shows the position - time $(x-t)$ graph of one-dimensional motion of a body of mass $0.4 \mathrm{~kg}$. The magnitude of each impulse is
question: The figure shows the position - time $(x-t)$ graph of one-dimensional motion of a body of mass $0.4 \mathrm{~kg}$. The magnitude of each impulse is
solution:
From the graph, it is a straight line so, uniform motion. Because of impulse direction of velocity changes as can be seen from the slope of the graph.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Initial velocity }=\frac{2}{2}=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \text { Final velocity }=-\frac{2}{2}=-1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \qquad \bar{P}_{\mathrm{i}}=0.4 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s} \\
& \qquad \overline{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{i}}=-0.4 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s} \\
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{J}}=\overline{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{i}}=-0.4-0.4=-0.8 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{J}}=\text { impulse }) \\
& |\overrightarrow{\mathrm{J}}|=0.8 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reason R : During elastic collision, the momentum and kinetic energy are both conserved.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:

Assertion A : Body P having mass M moving with speed u has head-on collision elastically with another body Q having mass m initially at rest. If m≪M, body Q will have a maximum speed equal to 2u after collision.

Reason R : During elastic collision, the momentum and kinetic energy are both conserved.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: A is correct but R is not correct.
A is not correct but R is correct.
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
For e=1 & second body at rest

V2=2m1u1 m1+m2=2uMM+m≃2u

Since M>>m

q: Two bodies of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> have position vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> respectively. The magnitude of position vector of centre of mass of this system will be similar to the magnitude of vector :
question: Two bodies of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> have position vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> respectively. The magnitude of position vector of centre of mass of this system will be similar to the magnitude of vector :
solution:
As we know, centre of mass is given by, r→com=∑mir→i∑mi

r→com=mı^+2ȷ^+k^+3m-3ı^-2ȷ^+k^m+3m

r→com=-8mı^-4mȷ^+4mk^4m=-2ı^-ȷ^+k^

The magnitude of the position vector of the centre of mass is 22+11+12=6.

It is similar to the magnitude of the option A.
